Defendant Cybernet Entertainment, Llc'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Further Responses To Special Interrogatories (Set Two)
Off Calendar Oct-12-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Sandra Ribera Speed, Esq. 415-576-1600 of Ribera Law Firm for Plaintiff John Doe.
Elizabeth Rhodes, Esq.650-989-5400 of Clapp Moroney Vucinich Beeman Scheley for Defendant Peter Acworth.
Justina Tate, Esq. 858-263-4070 of Tyson & Mendes for Defendant Cybernet.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT CYBERNET ENTERTAINMENT, LLC Defendant Cybernet Entertainment, Llc'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Further Responses To Special Interrogatories (Set Two)

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. A key issue in this case is whether plaintiff's participation in the May 3, 2013 filming of a sex video was a cause of his HIV infection. Asserting that it is highly probable he was infected from April 20 to May 13, 2013 (based on his doctor's hearsay opinion), plaintiff provided a list of his sexual partners in that time frame and the partners' HIV test results. Defendant Cybernet Entertainment, LLC, however, seeks to further compel discovery of such information from four months before and six weeks after May 3, 2013. Cybernet says, inter alia, that plaintiff's sex partners in those time periods "may be potential sources of HIV exposure." Plaintiff contends that Cybernet's discovery would be irrelevant and invade privacy.Because the "window of exposure" for HIV infection is within the ken of medical experts, I gave both sides the opportunity to submit expert declarations. "Based on a reasonable degree of medical certainty," plaintiff's expert puts the "window" at April 15, 2013 to May 7, 2013. Cybertnet's expert first places the opening date of the window at April 10, 2013, but then says testing variance could put it as early as March 16. Cybernet's expert puts the end date at May 12. Thus, all three doctors agree on the end date within six days: May 13, May 7, May 12 - and all those dates are within the time frame for which plaintiff has already provided discovery. There is also significant agreement on the start date: April 20, April 15, April 10 (though Cybertnet's expert offers two dates). Most importantly, none of the doctors adopts anything approaching the 5.5-month period for which Cybernet's motion demands discovery. The motion is DENIED.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Of Plaintiffs Unruh Act Allegations
Off Calendar Oct-05-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Atty. Sandra Ribera Speed of the Ribera aw Firm, Tel. No. 1-415-576-1600 appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty, Raymond K. Wilson of Tyson & Mendes, tel. No. 1-858-263-4136 appearing for the Defendant;
Atty. Elizabeth D. Rhodes of Clapp Moroney, Tel. No. 1-650-989-5400 appearing for the
Defendant Peter Acworth;
Atty. Richard W. Freeman Jr., of Smith Dollar,Tel. No. 1-707-921-3905 appearing for the
Defendant Hogan Karl.
RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT CYBERNET ENTERTAINMENT, LLC's MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION Of Plaintiffs Unruh Act Allegations - Defendant Cybernet Entertainment, LLC moves for summary adjudication of plaintiff's eleventh cause of action - Unruh Civil Rights Act (Civil Code 51 et seq.) - on six grounds. The motion is DENIED.Waiver by Conduct. Cybernet concedes "that no performer could participate in filming" of its "straight shoots" "without having first tested negative for STIs [sexually transmitted infections] and HIV," while the same was not true for its "gay shoots." Cybernet says "Plaintiff voluntarily chose to forego his right" to mandate HIV testing, but plaintiff says Cybernet never informed him of its "testing optional policy on gay shoots." (See PSSUMF 1, 17.) This creates an issue of material fact for trial. (See CCP 437c.)
Motivation. Cybernet claims its conduct was not "substantially motivated by its perception of" plaintiff's "sexual orientation," citing "input" from the "gay community" for its policies and "cross over" by performers between gay and straight shoots. However, Cybernet's policies are facially discriminatory, and motivations are often implied, so a triable issue exists. (See Koebke v. Bernardo Heights Country Club (2005) 36 Cal.4th 824, 854.)
Causation. Under the Unruh Act, injury is caused when a discriminatory policy is applied to the plaintiff, actual damage is not required. (Angelucci v. Century Supper Club (2007) 41 Cal.4th 160, 174.) Plaintiff need not have contracted HIV to have an Unruh Act claim.
Statute of Limitations. As previously ruled on Cybernet's demurrer, plaintiff's Unruh Act claim relates back to the original complaint's filing.
Workplace Discrimination. As also previously ruled on Cybernet's demurrer, the Unruh Act may apply to independent contractors such as plaintiff. (See Payne v. Anaheim Memorial Medical Center, Inc. (2005) 130 Cal.App.4th 729, 747-48.)
Written Waiver. Cybernet concedes that statutory damages are available to plaintiff, so its argument that plaintiff waived actual damages fails to dispose of the Unruh Act claim and summary adjudication is unavailable. (See DeCastro West Chodorow & Burns, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 410, 422.) Order signed in open court. Justina Tate, Esq. 858-459-4400 of Tyson Mended for Defendant Cybernet Entertainment.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT CYBERNET ENTERTAINMENT, LLC Defendant Cybernet Entertainment, Llc'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff'S Further Responses To Special Interrogatories (Set Two).

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. A key issue in this case is whether plaintiff's participation in the May 3, 2013 filming of a sex video was a cause of his HIV infection. Asserting that it is highly probable he was infected from April 20 to May 13, 2013, plaintiff has provided a list of his sexual partners in that time frame and the partners' HIV test results. Defendant Cybernet Entertainment, LLC, however, seeks to further compel discovery of such information from four months before and six weeks after May 3, 2013. Cybernet says, inter alia, that plaintiff's sex partners in those time periods "may be potential sources of HIV exposure." Plaintiff contends that Cybernet's discovery would be irrelevant and invade privacy.

The "window of exposure" for HIV infection is within the ken of medical experts. Plaintiff relies on hearsay, including a doctor's letter. Cybernet adduces no on-point evidence. Both parties may submit expert declarations on this issue under penalty of perjury. This shall be done on or before October 5, 2018. Hearing of the pending discovery motion is CONTINUED to October 12, 2018 at 9:30 a.m.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT CYBERNET ENTERTAINMENT, LLC'S DEMURRER TO 3RD AMENDED COMPLAINT - Defendant Cybernet Entertainment, LLC's demurrer to the eleventh cause of action for violation of the Unruh Act is overruled. The statute of limitations does not bar plaintiff's Unruh Act claim as it relates back to the filing of the original complaint. The Unruh Act claim is based on the same general set of facts as plaintiff's other claims. The original, first amended, and second amended complaints all include allegations regarding the disparate treatment between homosexual and heterosexual actors. (Complaint, par. 28; First Amended Complaint, par. 31; Second Amended Complaint, par. 54.) The claims involve the same injury. The discriminatory conduct allegedly led to plaintiff's contraction of HIV. Cybernet's argument that the Unruh Act claim fails because that Act does not apply to workplace discrimination claims lacks merit. Per Payne v. Anaheim Memorial Medical Center (2005) 130 Cal.App.4th 729, the Unruh Act can apply to an independent contractor such as plaintiff. Order signed in open court. Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings On Plaintiffs First Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Apr-03-2018 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Elizabeth D. Rhodes, Esq. of Clapp Moroney Vucinich Beeman Scheley, T:650.989.5400, for defendant/moving party Peter Acworth;
Sandra Ribera Speed, Esq. of Ribert Law Firm, T:415.576.1600, for plaintiff/opposing party;
Susan L. Oliver, Esq. of Tyson Mendes, T:858.263.4134, for defendant Cybernet Entertainment LLC;
Ronald Berestka, Esq. of Stone & Associates, T:925.938.1555, for defendant Armory Studios LLC.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopts the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT PETER ACWORTH's MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS ON PLAINTIFF'S FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as follows:

Defendant Peter Acworth moves for judgment on the pleadings of the first amended complaint (FAC). The motion is DENIED in its entirety. Count 2: Negligence per se requires separate jury instructions (CACI 418-421) and is often pled as a separate count from negligence. California is a notice-pleading jurisdiction; defendants have notice of the negligence per se theory. Count 3: Intentional misrepresentation is adequately pled. Defendants' arguments regarding reliance are improper at the pleading stage. Count 4: Conspiracy to commit fraud is adequately pled. A complaint may plead in the alternative and acts by individual defendants are alleged. (See, e.g., FAC 23:11-15.) Count 5: Breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing is adequately pled. A safe work site is pled as a benefit of the contract. (FAC 26:1-19.) Count 8: Intentional infliction of emotional distress is adequately pled. (See, e.g., FAC 34:17-35:2.) Defendant's argument that plaintiff was aware of the risks is improper at the pleading stage. Duty/Assumption of Risk: Defendants' arguments are improper at the pleading state. Whether the assumption of risk doctrine applies is determined after evidentiary inquiry. Individual Liability: It is alleged that Acworth (1) personally directed and actively participated in the tortious conduct and (2) made the policy decisions that caused an unsafe photo shoot. (See, e.g., FAC 11:1-3, 34:17-35:6.)

Counsel for plaintiff is to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and also complies with CCP 437c(g) and submit it in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

A complaint may plead in the alternative and acts by individual defendants are alleged. (See, e.g., FAC 23:11-15.) Count 5: Breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing is adequately pled. A safe work site is pled as a benefit of the contract. (FAC 26:1-19.) Count 8: Intentional infliction of emotional distress is adequately pled. (See, e.g., FAC 34:17-35:2.) Defendant's argument that plaintiff was aware of the risks is improper at the pleading stage. Duty/Assumption of Risk: Defendants' arguments are improper at the pleading state. Whether the assumption of risk doctrine applies is determined after evidentiary inquiry.

Counsel for plaintiff is to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling and also complies with CCP 437c(g) and submit it in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Atty. SANDRA RIBERA SPEED, ESQ. of Ribera Law Firm, Tel No. 415-576-1600 appearing for the Plaintiff;
Atty. JOHN C. ELLIS, ESQ. of Kennick & Associates, Tel No. 714-992-6600 appearing for Defendants Kink Studios, LLC, Cybernet Entertainment, LLC, Armory Studios, LLC, Peter Acworth, and Hogan Karl.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Defendants' demurrer is overruled in its entirety. Defendants' request for judicial notice is granted.. Paragraph 21 of the First Amended Complaint (FAC) alleges that plaintiff was an independent contractor. The court must accept that this allegation as true. Nothing in the FAC or that is judicially noticeable establishes that plaintiff was an employee. The determination whether one is an employee or independent contractor is done on a case by case factual involving the consideration of numerous factors. (S.G. Borello & Sons, Inc. v. State Dept. of Indus Relations (1989) 48 Cal.3d. 341). The Cal OSHA Appeals Board Decision does not establish that plaintiff was an employee in this particular case. Nor does plaintiff's workers' compensation claim establish that plaintiff was an employee. The status of the worker's compensation proceeding is unclear and it is premature to determine whether judicial estoppel applies. Moreover, the facts alleged in the FAC support exceptions to the exclusivity rule. (Labor 3601(a)(1) (injury caused by the willful and unprovoked physical act of aggression of another employee); 3602(b)(1) (willful physical assault)& (2) (fraudulent concealment aggravates injury)). The alleged OSHA violations support plaintiff's negligence per se claim. (Elsner v. Uveges (2004) 34 Cal.4th 915, 928). ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT.
